Even so it makes the words clear and stops the way. So the best answer is to use the idea that showing you are good at your job helps your talk with true words. So my answer is: The best thing to do is to let the person who made the talk have good reasons to make others believe they did well.

english_query: counterexample to: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species, (ii) B appears without prior learning or training, and (iii) B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern that does not vary across individuals or occasions.
ugf_response: First, we see that people do not know their name or how they spend money. This means they cannot teach others in a perfect way. Then, we look at the idea that a plan must work well for any person, no matter what they think others will do. But someone might say that being nice is not enough because you need a good thing that does not happen often. Even so, the main point is that if people do not learn from bad choices, they will fail to give good things to others. So this means we must keep working together to fix problems. So my answer is: a plan is good only when everyone knows each other and can change what they do. If people are not ready, it is not a good plan for anyone.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P trusts a person Q if and only if (i) P and Q are personally acquainted — that is, P has direct knowledge of Q's character gained through repeated personal interaction, (ii) P has observed Q acting reliably in the past and infers that Q will act reliably in the future, and (iii) the trust is reciprocal — Q also trusts P.
ugf_response: First we see two people each can give help or not give help. Then we note they both know that if one gives help the other can get help back and if one does not give help the other loses the good thing. Next the two good things they could get later do well because they know they will meet again which makes it more safe to keep help. But someone might say that taking away help now could be better than getting more later so there is no strong reason to think the other side the idea of just right now would hold. Still the chance that they will see each other again and may talk is strong enough to make them choose to give help. a possible reason for another view is that maybe they think the other might take more than give, or that the way things are done changes what they want and that can cause one to feel bad later. Even with those reasons the chance of getting help back later is a big part of why they try hard. So my answer is: So the two good thing is a lot of hope for help and a chance of not giving help if they think the group will meet again and not have to keep help now.
